Disney Toy Story Signature Collection Buzz Lightyear Talking Action Figure Description change to:The Toy Story Andy's Room Buzz Lightyear Talking Action Figure is 12" tall. This is the most accurate Buzz Lightyear ever produced! He is a highly detailed deluxe film replica from DISNEY/PIXARs digital data. Fully poseable with over 32 points of articulation including fingers, wrists, elbows, shoulders, waist, ankles, knees and legs! Push red oval chest button for classic "Buzz Toy" phrases. Press blue oval chest button and he responds as "Space Ranger" Buzz with moving head action! He even knows if youre talking loudly. Talk to Buzz and he'll respond to your voice in Talk Back Mode by using the green oval button for ON/OFF Talk Back feature. Press red round chest button for flying mode with pop-out wing action and light-up wing tips. The level sensor knows when Buzz is in a horizontal flying position or standing vertically and responds with flying/landing sound effects. You can also press his arm button for laser light-up and sound effects. Open or close his helmet or arm communicator and he reacts with related phrases and sound effects. He has over 60 sayings in original voice. The Toy Story Andy's Room Buzz Lightyear Talking Action Figure has a soft rubber waist and glow-in-the-dark green trim. He also includes Toy Story Collection Certificate of Authenticity.

Buzz Lightyear, Space Ranger! Is he real or just a toy - you decide! I'm pretty sure you know who Buzz Lightyear is but if not he's a space ranger who's a toy, or a toy who's a space ranger... Just watch a Toy Story Movie already! This is the Toy Story Collection Buzz Lightyear and just... Wow.

For kids and adults:
You may look at the price tag and think, "Wow, that's expensive for a Buzz Lightyear," and certainly there are less expensive versions but this is THE Buzz to own whether you are a player (with toys) or a collector. First of all the box... I'm not a keep in the box kind of guy and usually just chunk `em but this box is an exacting (aside from some real world concessions) replica of the boxes Buzz comes in in the movies. Smartly the wings are not attached in the store (imagine the carnage) but are included so you can attach at home for display or when giving to that Pixar loving kid on Christmas day. A very nice box for a mass market toy.

This guy is a huge and bulky 12" figure and is impeccably sculpted to match the movie. From the bottom of his feet, pointed knees, rubbery waist up to his cocky face with arched eyebrow this guy is Buzz from head to toe. His backpack and wings are also picture perfect.

Paint is fairly simple but all clean. The work on his face, eyes, eyebrows, mouth... all look great and clean. The only slop at all is the around the edges of the purple tips on his fingers and the green patches on his hands. And speaking of the green - IT'S GLOW IN THE DARK! He has a clean green color used to highlight many parts of his body, the biggest area being his chest, but you wouldn't even know it glows until you get him in the dark it's such a normal looking paint. One downside is that some details, like warnings on his jetpack and his communicator and space ranger symbol, are stickers. It looks fine and is movie accurate but as stickers do they will peel over time and play.

Articulation is all high quality. The head is part of the features but can be moved when he's not on but I would not recommend excessive tampering with it. Ball shoulders and elbows offer smooth and stable positioning, the wrists turn and the four fingers on each hand move at the hand knuckle. The only down side to the arms is they cannot hang straight down from the shoulder - but they look so good with hand on hips or in a more action pose it's hardly missed. His waist is clicky with a couple of clicks to lean him front and back and side to side that add a lot of pose-ability. His hip joints have a full and smooth range of movement (forward, back and out), the knees are clicky and also rotate (clicky too) and the ankles have a small range. It may not sound like a lot but it really is perfect for this toy and more than you might expect and the quality of the joints is top notch. There's a little wobble but nothing a steady hand can't manage.

Special features? This guy is ALL special features. In toy mode he has a few phrases to say and standard wing pop and fold and helmet open and close and laser sounds. Also his voice and sounds are of a more tinny and old fashioned quality. But in Space Ranger mode he's much more interactive - or that is to say he has a lot more to say. His head turns side to side and as the box says, over 65 phrases, and add that to the lights (arm laser and wing tips) and sounds. His wings pop out at the push of a button and make appropriate noises both popping out and folding in and they do both well. The helmet opens and closes and makes noises and dialog (depending on mode) as appropriate. His laser lights up and makes movie accurate sound and his communicator opens and closes with appropriate sounds and dialog as well. Add on to that with wings out you can fly him in a horizontal fashion for swooping and swooshing sounds and when you make him vertical again he makes landing sounds and appropriate dialog you've got a feast of special features. Oh, did I mention he has a microphone and listens out for you as well and responds to noises you make? If you stay quiet he'll ask where you are and eventually enter "hypersleep" mode. The only possible downside is that, yes, some of the sounds and dialog are sequential, but there are so many and they are subject to several factors that it's hardly a bother.

A couple of things in leaving. One, for the price this guy does a lot. Compare to a leader class Transformer that may have 3 sounds and the only other advantage is transformation (but Buzz does have pop out wings and open and closeable helmet and communicator and glow in the dark paint) and the tiny bit more money translates to a lot of extra features. Two, this is such a collector toy that it comes with that great box and an actual certificate of authenticity while at the same time being economical and fun enough to be a great toy for kids too. And last, the box does not lie... I've easily cataloged 64 different sayings that Buzz says and I'm sure I either missed some or they counted some in different circumstances but he really does say a lot and makes many sounds and has lights and all. Because I know many would prefer the sayings to be a surprise I won't list here, but the first (maybe only) comment here will be me publishing all the sayings I've found (because some will want to know) so consider that a spoiler warning on the comments section. Oh and um, 5 out of 5, OK?

Because I know many would prefer the sayings to be a surprise I won't list here, but the first (maybe only) comment here will be me publishing all the sayings I've found (because some will want to know) so consider that a spoiler warning on the comments section. Oh and um, 5 out of 5, OK?

Disney Toy Story Signature Collection Buzz Lightyear Talking Action Figure Description change to:The Toy Story Andy's Room Buzz Lightyear Talking Action Figure is 12" tall. This is the most accurate Buzz Lightyear ever produced! He is a highly detailed deluxe film replica from DISNEY/PIXARs digital data. Fully poseable with over 32 points of articulation including fingers, wrists, elbows, shoulders, waist, ankles, knees and legs! Push red oval chest button for classic "Buzz Toy" phrases. Press blue oval chest button and he responds as "Space Ranger" Buzz with moving head action! He even knows if youre talking loudly. Talk to Buzz and he'll respond to your voice in Talk Back Mode by using the green oval button for ON/OFF Talk Back feature. Press red round chest button for flying mode with pop-out wing action and light-up wing tips. The level sensor knows when Buzz is in a horizontal flying position or standing vertically and responds with flying/landing sound effects. You can also press his arm button for laser light-up and sound effects. Open or close his helmet or arm communicator and he reacts with related phrases and sound effects. He has over 60 sayings in original voice. The Toy Story Andy's Room Buzz Lightyear Talking Action Figure has a soft rubber waist and glow-in-the-dark green trim. He also includes Toy Story Collection Certificate of Authenticity.

Buzz Lightyear, Space Ranger! Is he real or just a toy - you decide! I'm pretty sure you know who Buzz Lightyear is but if not he's a space ranger who's a toy, or a toy who's a space ranger... Just watch a Toy Story Movie already! This is the Toy Story Collection Buzz Lightyear and just... Wow.

For kids and adults:
You may look at the price tag and think, "Wow, that's expensive for a Buzz Lightyear," and certainly there are less expensive versions but this is THE Buzz to own whether you are a player (with toys) or a collector. First of all the box... I'm not a keep in the box kind of guy and usually just chunk `em but this box is an exacting (aside from some real world concessions) replica of the boxes Buzz comes in in the movies. Smartly the wings are not attached in the store (imagine the carnage) but are included so you can attach at home for display or when giving to that Pixar loving kid on Christmas day. A very nice box for a mass market toy.

This guy is a huge and bulky 12" figure and is impeccably sculpted to match the movie. From the bottom of his feet, pointed knees, rubbery waist up to his cocky face with arched eyebrow this guy is Buzz from head to toe. His backpack and wings are also picture perfect.

Paint is fairly simple but all clean. The work on his face, eyes, eyebrows, mouth... all look great and clean. The only slop at all is the around the edges of the purple tips on his fingers and the green patches on his hands. And speaking of the green - IT'S GLOW IN THE DARK! He has a clean g
top_rant.title: Amazing!

top_rant.text:
I ordered this Quadcopter on Nov. 11 and received it...and the case I bought at the same time, on Friday, the 15th...very speedy service on these items. I had read quite a bit about the DJI Phantom Quads, and once my Vision was on the way, I tried to read all of the info I could get my hands on....I need to say that the "Tutorials" on the DJI Web Site were extremely helpful, very positive and supportive about all phases of the operation of this amazing flying machine.

I need to say here, that I had Zero experience flying any sort of model aircraft, no time using any sort of radio control or really, very little contact with anyone in the RC community...so, I was really a total novice...and fairly apprehensive about learning to "drive" one of these expensive toys....without a major hiccup...! So, why did I buy this Quad?? My main interest is in photography and the current issue of Outdoor Photography features an article on using one of these devices for aerial photos. Who has not wished they could "get a camera way up there!"....or "zoom over the steeple of that church"...or "see what this neighborhood looks like from 300 feet up".? When I read the specs of the Vision camera...14meg, stills or video, jpeg and RAW, intervalometer...a long list..I knew I'd get a lot of use out of it. Just the idea of being able to rotate the Quad while shooting multiple still images for panos was enough to make the final decision for me...!

Anyway...Fri. the boxes started to arrive and finally, I got the opportunity to see the Phantom in 'real life'.. I had watched the "unpacking" tutorial from DJI, so there were no surprises with any of this. I pulled out the main battery and the range extender and started them charging. (Main battery took about an hour...the extender was longer than that...3-4 hours..FYI) I took that time to do the few things required...spin on the props, apply the 'racing stripes', attach the range extender and the iDevice holder to the range extender, check the new carry case...and worry some more..!!

Both weekend days were extremely windy...up to 50+ MPH...well in excess of what any sane person would want to use as beginner training for this fairly complex device. Monday was a different story...temps in the 60's, and only gentle breezes..5-8MPH at most. So......the case with the Phantom and the rest of the gear went into the mini-van...and we headed for a local park. It took only a few minutes to turn on the range extender, let it establish the WiFi link, go to "Settings" on my iPhone...(4s...FYI)...open the DJI 'app', see that it had connected successfully....and head to a convenient bench nearby. There I powered up the battery in the Phantom itself, turned on the Controller power, flipped the S1 switch about 9 times to get it into Compass Configuration mode...did the required rotations with the Phantom and got the correct response from it...good to see the green LED's confirming that all was ready. I checked the iPhone app again that I had pre-loaded a week earlier...removed the lens cap..(very important!)...and Bingo...everything looked great... I placed the Phantom about 20 feet away from the bench....pulled the CSC joysticks to the "Motors On" position, watched the motors come to life...(I had done this at home a few days earlier....so I knew what to expect..!), took a deep breath....(a few spectators had gathered by this time...always good to try something totally new in front of a crowd..!!)...and waited for the Moment of Truth....fingers crossed...heart beat full on.....hoping for the best...

I advanced the left joy stick slowly.....I heard and saw the motors speed up....a bit more stick...and then...smoothly...almost as if I actually knew what I was doing....the Phantom rose into the air...about 6-7 feet at that point...WOW!!....did I say WOW?...!! It worked...just as advertised...so easy, not a single bobble...nothing but the comforting sound of the motors and the sight of the Phantom just hovering in the air....stable...the red and green LEDs under the arms flashing...a picture on my iPhone from the on-board camera....and a host of approving....and amazed comments from the peanut gallery..! The phrase, "That is SO cool!" was heard more than once...! Flushed with my new success, I advanced the left joy stick again...and the Phantom responded by rising another 8-10 feet into the air...it really had such and 'other-worldly' look to it...just floating in the air...steady...only the prop noise giving any hint that it was there... Since the camera was pointed away from us, I moved the left stick to the side and watched the Phantom rotate and at the same time I could see the display on my iPhone show the view from 15 feet up....turning...90 degrees, more....then...our small bunch of observers came into view...more use of the "Cool" word...!

Since things were going so well, I started moving the right hand joy stick...the Phantom mirrored each command...forward...then back...to the left...then back to the right... Quite frankly, I was totally amazed by how easy this had all gone...My confidence level had gone from merely hoping I could get this off the ground and not embarrass myself...to feeling pretty totally in control of this device and the attached camera....which was confirming each and every action of the Phantom itself..Wheeeeeee...!! Since I knew the main battery was good for about 25+ minutes, I flew the Phantom Vision up higher...rotated it....went a few hundred yards away, turned back...lower...this is getting addictive...! When I got it closer, I handed the Controller to my wife...and she had no trouble with the basic controls either...I'd like think that it was my skilled take-off that was the basis for her ability to maneuver the craft...but I know better..!! The next 20 minutes were spent in flying around the general area...flexing my aviation muscles, so to speak....I always kept it in view...although having the visual confirmation on the iPhone screen will allow operation that would be out of sight of the operator...another time maybe...! Pushing the left joy stick forward...caused the Phantom to begin to climb...at about a hundred feet up, it became a tiny dot in the sky....another hundred and it was nearly invisible...the lights of the LED's could not be seen then due to the sunny skies...but...we knew it was there...! It was a strange, but satisfying, feeling to be in control of this "eye in the sky"....close to euphoria...!!

Now I HAVE been around many aircraft....both full size...and models, enough to know that the number of landings should exactly equal the number of take-offs....so this would be my final challenge...although I really had no fear about this going wrong after the 20+ minutes of experience (Old Hand..!)...I had in the air. Bringing the Quad back to the take-off point, I settled it to a point about 5 feet above the grass, pulled back on the left hand joy stick...the Phantom moved down....touched the ground....a second later, I could hear the motors spin down...(I had kept the left joy stick in the full rear...or Power Off..position)...and...accepted the accolades of the crowd....OK...the 4 or 5 people who were there..!!

Final view of all of this?? Incredible to see this machine hover at any place you want...just remove your fingers from the sticks and it STOPS...the few times I was unsure of the orientation of the Panther, I did just that...figured out what I wanted to do next...and then continued. This is unlike an airplane...which continues flying while the pilot searches for his (MY..!!)...next move.. EASY TO FLY?? Yes....very...a super solid, comfortable, controlling experience. I can't wait to put this to photographic uses...having a mobile camera platform that can basically be placed within about a quarter of a mile and up to 400 feet in the air...(more if out of range of an airport...)...and the ability to switch from stills to video...to aim the camera up or down...0 degrees to 60 degrees of motion, able even to format the micro SD card in flight..!! Rotating this Phantom as I trigger the camera for some panos should be amazing....stay tuned...!!

If interested in following this tale...I have just started a Group on Flickr...[...]
top_rant.title: Totally AMAZING does not even come close...!!

top_rant.text:
THE FACTS: I was able to get this Cut and Style Barbie doll (1994 / 1995) for a fairly reasonable price; she arrived in a reasonable amount of time, and her box was clean and bright!

NOTE: The particular doll that I received DID NOT arrive in the multi-lingual box that is pictured on Amazon's sales page, so if you want THAT EXACT DOLL, please contact the seller, because, unless they specify that their particular doll arrives in a multi-lingual box, she'll PROBABLY arrive in an English-language box.

The red-haired version of Cut and Style Barbie IS INTENDED FOR CHILDREN AGED 5 AND UP, and she arrives with an Instructions Guide, a Cool Cuts and Styles Guide, a velcro-backed brunette hairpiece, A SUPER COOL "Barbie for Girls!" POSTER (As far as I know, ONLY the red-haired version of Cut and Style Barbie arrives with this poster), a pair of green child-sized scissors, a green brush, four rubber bands, a neon orange fabric headband, a neon pink fabric headband, a ruffled (neon yellow) headband, and a wide headband (neon pink, adorned with neon yellow tulle and a green curlicue ribbon)

BARBIE'S BODY, FACE, AND HAIR: Red-haired Cut and Style Barbie sports the typical, tan Twist N' Turn Barbie body of the mid-1990s (permanently bent arms, bendable legs, and molded-on, pink, "B" logo underwear)

Her face may have darkened slightly, due to her age (She's about 20 years old), but she looks rather interesting, with her reddish-brown eyebrows, her striking blue eyes, her avocado green eye shadow, her rosy pink blush, and her bright red lips!

Her hair reaches about the middle of her calves, and it's a medium, attractive shade of spice-like red! If I flatten her bangs against her forehead, they reach her eyes, but they tend to have a lot of body, and so they have a shape of their own, you might say.

BARBIE'S HEAD, AND THE HAIRPIECE: The very back of Barbie's head features a small, horizontal area that's not rooted, and, directly below THAT is a fuzzy, deep red area, to which the hairpiece can be attached.

To attach Barbie's hairpiece to her head, simply peel the attached, protective layer of fabric back from the velcro, flip Barbie over, flip her hair out of the way, position the velcro strip on top of the top of the fuzzy area, smooth the protective fabric down over the hairpiece, and flip Barbie's hair back over the hairpiece. Said hairpiece is AMAZINGLY STRONG, as it CAN BE BRUSHED WITHOUT falling off! I was SURPRISED...

BARBIE'S OUTFIT: Red-haired Cut and Style Barbie wears a TWO-PIECE, neon green outfit of smart, flirty proportions! Her embossed-looking, shiny, sleeveless green top (which features three very smart-looking, gold buttons) fits snugly over a flared, solid green skirt, underneath which are attached a matching tulle slip and a pair of sewn-in underwear. Barbie's shoes are neon green, Minnie Mouse-type pumps, and she completes her fashion look with a gold ring (on her RIGHT hand, of course), gold and black dangle earrings, AND an ultra-elegant black and gold choker necklace! She looks sooo ELEGANT!

BARBIE'S ACCESSORIES, ETC.: Barbie's accessories consist of a red hairpiece, a green brush, a green pair of child-sized scissors, a pink cloth ribbon, an orange cloth ribbon, a ruffly yellow headband, and a well-adorned pink headband.

NOTE: I don't think that I will ever cut the hair of ANY Cut and Style Barbie, so I cannot comment about how well the scissors work, OR about how good or bad Barbie's hair looks once it's been cut.

THE GOOD: The "style" aspect of Cut and Style Barbie actually WORKS! When Barbie wears her hairpiece, it's STRONG ENOUGH that I can STILL BRUSH IT, and it DOESN'T fall off, like some cheap piece of trash. Even though the hairpiece IS a little tough to pull off, it at LEAST WORKS REALLY WELL, AND you can "close" it, too, but covering the velcro strip with the attached, protective cloth strip whenever you want to put the hairpiece away for later.

That having been said, ALL of the Cut and Style Barbie dolls are just ATTRACTIVE OVERALL. I love their big, heavily-lined eyes, their unusual, flirty, two-piece outfits, the elegant look of their earrings, AND ESPECIALLY their cool, old-fashioned-looking chokers, which I've read were popular during the early-to-mid 1990s!

I really love the red-haired Cut and Style Barbie doll's unusual, striking beauty. She's a TURQUOISE-EYED REDHEAD, which makes her rare in the Barbie world, and I think that her red lips make her even more beautiful!

THE BAD: Barbie's hairpiece tends to come undone if it's brushed when it's flipped forward...over the top of Barbie's head, and Barbie's hair can lose its curl after it's brushed.

This Barbie is a mid-1990s Barbie doll, so her face, and ESPECIALLY her LEGS, may become ever so slightly tacky-feeling (That is the case with my particular Barbie). Sometimes, NRFB dolls CAN be in worse condition than long-ago-de-boxed dolls.

Some of her hair will probably fall out when it's first brushed, so be careful when you're brushing her hair. All of that hair may also become overwhelming for you, because there's so MUCH of it there that you may sometimes feel as though you're dealing with Cousin It, or whatever. LOL! That's the cost of having a Barbie doll with such long, beautiful hair, though. :)

Her hair might become stuck to the fuzzy portion of her head at times, but it seems as though that's a fairly easy problem to correct.

I can't vouch for the future state of this Barbie's outfit, as some of the fabrics that were used to create it might not age too well, but, for now, the outfit seems to be holding up well.

Barbie's bangs tend to "want" to be styled in a certain way, so it may be a wee bit difficult to coax them into the type of style that YOU want them to hold; her choker can also be annoyingly LOOSE around her neck, and her earrings can TOTALLY get in the way of any attempts at styling her hair. I chose to cut her earrings out for that very reason.

Her face can be ever so slightly darker than her body, and it can be ever so slightly tough, or almost too easy to rotate (Some of these issues effect my doll)

THE UGLY: Barbie's hair, and her face, can be issues. Her hair can be thin, and it can also be choppy at the bottom, due to quality control issues. My particular Barbie has super thin hair up on top, right next to her bangs, and her bangs were ALSO rooted TOO FAR UP on one side, SO THAT THE ROOTING LINE SHOWS! BOOO! Barbie's extra rubber bands will ALSO be unable to be used, due to their age, so PLEASE THROW THEM AWAY if you give them to a child.

This particular Barbie (The one that I got, anyway) is having problems with both the back of her head, AND with her hairpiece. The hair is already FALLING OUT OF THE HAIRPIECE, and the fuzzy area is COMING UNGLUED from her head! ARG!

Her face can look sort of weird, too, due to the exact same types of issues. My doll has some lip color on her teeth. My Barbie's eyebrows are truly positioned unevenly, and her eyes themselves have issues. They are slightly unevenly-sized, unevenly-painted, and slightly unevenly-positioned.

The MOST OFFENSIVE aspect of these dolls is the fact that they do NOT REALLY look like the promotional pictures that grace their boxes, with regards to their eye shadow. The box backs make it seem as though these dolls sport almost NONEXISTENT, LIGHT eye shadow, when, in fact, the color saturation of their eye shadow is ALL OVER THE PLACE! My particular doll is wearing avocado green eye shadow, but it's MUCH HEAVIER on one eye than it is on the other, and it's ALSO UNEVENLY-POSITIONED!

In ADDITION to all of these issues, my particular doll was wearing sewn-in underwear that were so badly sewn to the tulle slip that her skirt was fitting weirdly; I chose to remove both the underwear AND the tulle slip! Bad quality control, Mattel! Bad quality control!

RECOMMENDATIONS: I think that this Cut and Style doll would be perfect for...

1.) CHILDREN 5 AND UP: Little kids LOVE to play with doll hair, and this doll certainly has enough hair to please young stylists! I cannot vouch for the ease with which the included scissors can be used, though, because, at this time, I don't intend to cut my Cut and Style dolls' hair.

2.) RED-HAIRED / BLUE-EYED BARBIE FANS: This barbie may be perfect for those who have reddish hair of any shade, and / or for those who just love red-haired Barbie dolls! Remember that a lot of red-haired children secretly WISH that they could have a Barbie doll that had the same hair color that they have. In addition, few actual BARBIE dolls themselves have red hair AND turquoise blue eyes, so this Barbie could be perfect for that red-haired, blue-eyed someone who wants a Barbie doll that shares their coloration, and / OR for that Barbie enthusiast who wants a blue-eyed Barbie doll that's NOT a BLONDE.

3.) GIFT-RECIPIENTS: This lovely, red-haired Cut and Style Barbie could be the perfect gift for a Barbie collector, a Barbie enthusiast, or a child! She'd work well as a Birthday gift, and she'd be cool as a Valentine's Day surprise, or as a custom Easter basket gift. She'd also make a great Mother's Day gift for that mom who's also a doll enthusiast, and she'd be a super "treat" for that special someone on Halloween, due to her neon-look outfit! Of course, she'd also work well for a Winter holiday gift. Just prepare yourself for the screams of delighted surprise that you'll hear!

4.) A RED-HAIRED BIRTHDAY GIRL: This red-haired Barbie sports a particularly striking, interesting brand of beauty, and so she might make a lovely gift for that red-haired Birthday girl who also loves collecting / displaying Barbie dolls.

5.) ANY BIRTHDAY KID: Top your child's Birthday cake with this Barbie doll, and be sure to position her hair so that it DOESN'T touch the cake (LOL!), and listen for the "Ooo"s and the "Aaah"s when your child...and their companions see the cake!

IN CONCLUSION: This Cut and Style Barbie doll isn't perfect, but she's elegant, and she's lovely in an unusual, smart-looking way, so if you want to treat yourself to a pretty Barbie doll, she might be the perfect doll for you!
top_rant.title: Red-haired Cut and Style Barbie (1994 / 1995) is a fairly striking beauty, with her red hair, bright blue eyes, and red lips!

A neuronphaser.com review.

CONTENT(5/5)

The Dungeon Masters Guide is always a tough book to review, and I bet that’s because it’s an even tougher book to write. You’ve got new folks coming to D&D for the first time, sophomores coming from just one other edition of D&D to this new one (and “edition” in D&D terms is like the differences between an apple and psychometry: just totally different!), and you’ve got the grognards and edition warriors coming out of the mists of legend to check out the new beast on the block. It has to serve so many masters, cover so many topics, and somehow channel the best of rules mechanics, guidelines, functional tips, and enforcing social contracts. You almost can’t win.

The folks who wrote 5th Edition's DMG pulled out all the stops, and they succeeded.

Part 1: The Top-Down Approach

Part 1 of the DMG tackles the big-picture stuff, which is worth your perusal even if you run published campaign settings, as it covers a lot of ground from the nitty-gritty (Factions, languages, how settlements work) to the more theoretical (play style, overarching campaign-shattering events).

The first chapter is an exercise in taking the big-picture stuff about creating a campaign setting that looks and feels like a Dungeons & Dragons world, figuring out a playstyle that inspires both DM and Players with “I need to go on an adventure right now!” and doing it all in about as few words as possible. It’s successful for the most part, at its best when covering the importance and particulars of nailing down an accessible tone and world so players can show up at your table and have fun gaming, but the brevity means that certain subjects — mapping a campaign world, Factions and the benefits of rising in Faction rank, and alternative campaign milieus — really become more about missed opportunities, forcing DMs that interested in world-building to seek out other resources. Granted, there are resources APLENTY on the intarwebz, so it’s hard to fault the book for that.

Chapter 2: Creating a Multiverse is really just a guide to the existing “Great Wheel” cosmology of Dungeons & Dragons, updated for 5th Edition with a couple of nods and advances born out of the massive 4th Edition retcon of the multiverse. It doesn’t do a lot in helping a DM create their own cosmology — certainly not like previous editions’ Manual of the Planes or similar titles — but it does greatly simplify the mechanical influences of bizarre planes and realms controlled by deities or foreign concepts. In this way, it is very inspirational: every plane receives a unique Planar Trait that can alter some mechanic, or how spells work, or something else that’s usually pretty big in scope, but without a ton of record-keeping.

Parts 2 & 3: The Moving Parts

Part 2 covers the “everyday” issues of adventures, starting with the structure and style of adventures, moving on to populating them with interesting Non-Player Characters (NPCs) and adventuring sites, stocking those sites with ways to kill the player characters, and then moving into downtime activities and rewards. It takes up the bulk of the book, and for good reason: not everybody’s going to be building their own campaign setting when D&D already has over a dozen of those available second-hand or through the Dungeon Master’s Guild, but every DM is going to need how to make an adventure work, whether it’s a published adventure or one they homebrew themselves.

Going chapter by chapter is silly, because we need to address the coolest thing in the DMG right now: TABLES! There are oodles and oodles of tables, few of which offer a ridiculous number of options, but all of which provide fantastic inspiration for creating every aspect of an adventure, so much so that you could literally leave every decision up to a random roll. In fact, that’s exactly what James at GeekDad does in a series of articles (look 'em up!). But if you like having total control, the options presented are married with the text in such a way that you can just as easily choose one of the entries or feel inspired by what’s there. Even if you do end up rolling a few things, it’s obvious there’s a conscious effort to keep each table complete unto itself, which keeps you from accidentally rolling up contradictory information or finding bizarre dead-end choices.

Need help breaking a story? Refer to the random plotlines, villain goals, and events. Flesh out your NPCs — whether they are villains, patrons, allies, or lowly tavernkeepers — with a bevy of tables. Introduce plot twists and come up with some evocative or generic encounters, all using tables as your final answer or as your inspirational guide.

And keep an open mind! If you’re not terribly in tune with random tables (or D&D in general), you may miss the fact that you can use many (most? all?) of these tables for many, many purposes besides what they say on the tin. The issue this creates is that you may find yourself rolling on the NPC Interaction Traits table and getting one of the 12 results over and over again, which seems like the opposite of fun. Except you could’ve just as easily taken that sagely NPC and said, “Hmmm, I bet rolling a Personality Trait on the table in the Player’s Handbook for the Sage Background would be a little more germane.” And you’d be right. And it’s not just an issue across books — thinking about what’s in the DMG vs. the PHB — but an issue internal to the DMG itself. NPC Villains alone have tables in the Creating Adventures chapter (under Location-Based Adventures) and in the Creating Nonplayer Characters chapter (under Villains, appropriately). The Random Dungeons chapter at the end of the book necessarily relies on the Creating Adventures chapter as well, regularly calling back to tables found nearly 200 pages previous. There’s no index of tables in the DMG (and certainly some organizational issues if you rely on tables during gameplay), but you can find one in the Optional Rules & Random Tables Index release at Dungeon Masters Guild (dmsguild.com).

Part 3 includes a chapter on Running the Game followed by the Dungeon Master’s Workshop. This is where you’re going to see the most variant and optional rules — the Workshop is ultimately just a collection of variants and options — but also how some really basic stuff works in play, which honestly I think would’ve been better served earlier in the book, as this stuff is absolutely necessary for running just about any adventuring situation ever.

Finally, there are appendices for Random Dungeons, Monster Lists, Maps, and Dungeon Master Inspiration.

The Good

One of the best parts of this book is the “How to Use This Book” section, which is one of those sections that people might glance right over, but which new DMs should read because of the ground it covers. Part of the reason a lot of new or prospective DMs get overwhelmed is because they are looking at the page count of the PHB and then see another 317 pages of DMG on top of that and think, “Dear god! I have to read all this?!” And the unqualified answer is actually, “Absolutely not, no!”

Running Forgotten Realms? Then pretty much the entirety of Part 1 of this book is barely worth a skim. Running the adventures for Adventurers League at your local gaming store? 98% of the stuff in this book that says “optional” or “variant” rules are only going to muddy the waters, confusing you about what’s necessary to run the fast and furious convention-style sessions that makes AL a great intro but a bad long-term campaign plan.

The point is, there’s so much in this book that gets covered and yet your typical Dungeon Master only needs to know a small fraction of it at any given time. To point to a fact, look at how much of the DMG is included in the Basic D&D rules. The answer is “encounter building rules” and “magic items.” And it’s not even all of the magic items.

1. Breadth of Coverage

We just mentioned the breadth of topics covered by the Dungeon Masters Guide; well, that breadth is a good thing. For example, there are 16 pages (p. 71-87) covering how to create adventures. This includes:
*Location-based adventures
*Event-based adventures
*Mystery adventures
*Intrigue adventures
*Shaking up the adventure with moral quandaries, plot twists, and side quests
*Character objectives (basically, win conditions for encounters outside of “kill everything”)
*Encounter building (individual encounters, daily budgets by level, etc.)
*Building random encounter tables

The breadth of subject matter when it comes to creating adventures is huge, yet in a relatively small amount of space the DMG proves how jam-packed it is. Keep in mind that there are multiple random tables to inspire or roll up some results (and explanations for those tables’ entries where necessary), as well as ample discussion for each topic. Putting on my “I’m new to this hat” is very difficult after 30+ years of playing D&D, but I really felt like they covered everything in enough detail to get things rolling right away. As an experienced DM, it was clear that they were all about providing functional advice and leaving out crazy conceptual gaming theory: this is D&D, after all, so if you’re not here to run a D&D game, forget about you! I very much see that as a positive thing.

2. Depth of Coverage

In such short spaces, the DMG is able to cover a subject with a lot of depth, too. In just 4 pages, you’ve got everything you’ll ever need to add value to Non-Player Characters that directly help or even join a party: followers, adventurer NPCs, optional rules for Loyalty, contacts, patrons, hirelings, and extras. That doesn’t even include NPC character creation, villains, and the adventure-creation specifics of how to use the villains you create. That’s a sickening amount of depth for every subject the DMG covers, and yet it’s there, and it’s good. Old hats won’t find much of anything missing (except the Random Harlot table!) and new DMs now have everything they could ever need to adjudicate this sort of thing without hauling extra books or requiring complex subsystems.

3. Brevity

I keep mentioning the “small amount of space” or “in just X pages!” for every subject in the DMG, and it’s because all of the writing takes the shortest path to cover each topic. To a degree, this might miss out on the nuances of some subsystems like the optional rules for Renown in the section on player character Factions, but the rules are succinct where there are rules, and the guidance, tips, and tricks are functional. New DMs have enough to deploy these rules and ideas at the table immediately, and experienced system-tinkerers have more than enough to start adding to things to their tastes.

4. The New “Cool”

Chapter 6: Between Adventures really feels new and fresh in a Dungeon Master’s Guide, compared to editions past (even if it’s not entirely true that this stuff is new). It covers linking adventures, campaign tracking tips, handling recurring expenses (first appearing in the Player’s Handbook), and new downtime activities for player characters to engage in (as well as how to create even more activities). Rarely has this stuff been codified in this fashion, using explicit underlying systems. The bad news is that it’s specific enough that it may offend some tastes (something every new iteration of D&D finds a way to do), but luckily it’s so exposed by being in this chapter that the systems can easily be modified. I assume this may create issues with how to balance Eberron’s Artificer class, but that remains to be seen.

The Bad

There are some things that are just plain old missing, or downright bad. As you’ll see, however, they are such an insignificant piece of the DMG pie that I didn’t have the heart to even knock off a star from the Content section of this review, so…that should tell you something. I’m the kinda guy who hates a perfect score because nothing in the world is perfect outside of videos of kittens and baby goats, but there ya have it. The DMG is that good...but not truly perfect and here’s why:

1. Stunts

In the 4th Edition Dungeon Master’s Guide (2008), there’s a near-legendary system on page 42 that covers stunts and improvised maneuvers/powers. It may very well have been one of those things that was way cooler to read than it was to implement, but it exposed the underlying assumptions about expected damage output, DCs, and the whole At-Will/Encounter/Daily Power format that 4th Edition D&D characters and monsters used. Such a thing isn’t to be found in the latest Dungeon Master’s Guide…at least not so blatantly.

With a little cleverness and some page-flipping, DMs can make use of the trap rules (featuring saving throw DCs and damage severity by level) and the new spell creation rules (which features single- and multi-target spell damage by spell level) to create a similar stunting system. Reading into the expected monster stats by challenge rating helps a little, too. All well and good, but it’d be nice to have seen a repeat in clear and certain terms, rather than having to hack something together and hope you remembered to carry the 1.

2. Trap Stat Blocks

Like the spells in the Player’s Handbook, traps have a barebones “stat block” that obscures most of the common traits in the body text, including:
*Wisdom (Perception) check to spot the mechanism of the trap.
*Intelligence (Investigation) or other DCs to pick up additional information about the trap (if any).
*DCs for checks used to disable the trap, as well as the consequences for using gear other than thieves’ tools.
*Damage, conditions, and Saving Throw DCs.

That’s pretty annoying, as most of that stuff is universal across traps. Instead, you have to read through paragraphs of text to pick this stuff out.

3. More Indexes!

The DMG offers several Monster Lists (we’ll chat more about this in a second) that act as a useful reference for stocking dungeons and ruins with monsters of the appropriate challenge rating, but with all of the topics that the DMG covers so well, it’s crappy that there aren’t even more of these types of indexes, or that they fall a little short. An index of all the random tables, a checklist of all the optional rules in the DMG and PHB, including page references on the Monster Lists…let’s just say that the guide is lacking on organizational tools, which is one more thing that tends to trip up new Dungeon Masters more than old. If you want new blood for the game, you gotta make it easier for people to step into the role of DM for a session, and a 317-page book filled with a thousand subjects and a billion random tables is a pain in the butt to argue as being “easy” even though it’s a fact that DMing kinda is easy. It’s a hard sell. Add on the page count of the PHB and the need to reference monsters in various sections (not all of them are in alphabetical order, since they may fall under “parent” entries like Animals or NPCs), and you’ve got yourself a perfect case for the DMG including good organizational tools. But it doesn’t.

The Weird

There’s some stuff that’s just a little…interesting.

1. Appendix B: Monster Lists

As I said, there’s a whole appendix with lists of monsters, first by environment, then by Challenge Rating. The environments listed are:
*Arctic
*Coastal
*Desert
*Forest
*Grassland
*Hill
*Mountain
*Swamp
*Underdark (subterranean)
*Underwater
*Urban

Make sure your adventures conform to that, and you’re good to go. If they don’t, it’s not hard to get familiar with the monsters and repurpose a table or build your own. All of these environments feature the monsters organized by Challenge Rating, so populating your wilderness and dungeon environments with appropriate or near-appropriate encounters is a cinch. Why this info isn’t repeated in an ecology section in the Monster Manual is beyond me, because that’s one of the (relatively few) faults with that book, but I digress.

Notice a big frickin’ problem? Yeah, it’s the lack of page numbers. Not a single monster has its page number noted next to it, which is a blessing if the Monster Manual gets laid out differently soon, but is a pain in the butt for GMs who refer to the lists but maybe aren’t familiar with the monsters that well, and thus have to go hunting down the monsters in the MM either by page, table of contents, or index. It’s not terrible, but it’s not good either. Grouping monsters by their parent entry, or at least moving Animals and NPCs to their own notation within a table might have been a good start, at least.

2. Appendix C: Maps

There’s a bunch of maps included in the back of the DMG, and they cover a wide gamut of places:
*Wind mill
*House
*A dungeon that looks vaguely similar to the Lost Mine of Phandelver (from the D&D Starter Set)
*A dungeon and cave complex featuring an underground river/lake
*Two coastal cities/towns, one with a very unique “multi-island, river delta” layout
*A cave complex
*A double-masted boat’s deck plans
*A dungeon partially collapsed into a ravine

These maps are cool and evocative, and probably useful in a lot of given situations. But they are surprising both for their placement — about 200 pages after various sections on mapping — and for what they don’t include, like temples, castles, a manor house, oversized dwellings (all of the noted dungeons are 5-foot square corridors and tend towards tight spaces with only a few rooms that are a bit more open), and maybe a few bigger overland maps at the campaign or region scales noted earlier in the DMG.

Now, I’m doing the book a slight disservice with this critique, because if you thumb through the book you will find that several pieces of artwork actually act as maps on the sly. There’s a great piece that is an isometric rendering of an entire walled city, and there map sections of the Forgotten Realms complete with hexes. But in most cases, these are placed as if they are pieces of artwork, and so you might have text or other images running through them, or parts of the map cut off entirely to make way for section breaks.

None of this is a huge loss for internet savvy folks who can google up a map in a fifth of a second, or experienced DMs or illustrators who conjure up their own creations. But for the new DMs, there will be instances where showing is better than telling, so I think it would’ve been better to cut back on some of the bigger art pieces throughout the book in order to make room for more of that sort of thing.

FORM (4/5)

Singing the praises of the physical format of the Dungeon Master’s Guide is easy. It’s a joy, bound in the protective seals of arcane spellcasters and printed lovingly with the positive energy of the Seven Heavens of Mount Celestia.

What?

I’m saying it’s awesome. Just awesome.

And here’s where we knock off our one and only star: the text is so easy to smudge! I’ve made ample use of the Monster Stats By Challenge table in the Dungeon Master’s Guide, but all it took was lightly rubbing my finger along one row of the table to reference something and the row beneath my finger smudged like the dickens. Whatever printing stuff they are doing, it’s inky and liquidy and quite frankly the same thing happened to me while reading Curse of Strahd, so obviously the quality control hasn’t improved yet.
top_rant.title: One of the best, most immediately useful D&D reference books for at-the-table use ever written!

top_rant.text:
THE FACTS: This lovely, Minimalist, elegant doll was reasonably-priced, and she arrived ahead of schedule, too, looking like a radiant, late 1990s, slightly Goth princess of fashion and romance!

IMPORTANT NOTES: Please note that this doll's box is dated 1998, which means that she was released during 1999; she is about 17-years-old; therefore, her accessories may not work properly, and her body WILL be stained a grey-blue color, because of her black bodysuit. Mattel claimed that this doll was appropriate for children who were 3-years-old and older.

THE MATTEL INSTRUCTIONS, NOTES, AND ADVERTISEMENTS INCLUDED IN THE BOX: Mattel included a lot of papers in the box with this cool Very Velvet Teresa. First of all, there's a note about the included markers, which reads: "IMPORTANT: COLOR LIGHTLY. ALLOW MARKER INK TO DRY BEFORE REMOVING STICKERS FROM PAPER BACKING." There's also a "Barbie" advertising booklet from (dated 1998), which highlights some cool for-kids Barbie items that were available at the time, like an answering machine, a clock radio, and walkie talkies (They still HAD those cool things during the late 1990s? COOL!) The most IMPORTANT "note" that is included with Teresa is the Very Velvet Barbie Doll and Doll Friends Instructions booklet, which details the items that should be inside of the box, and how to use them.

TERESA'S BODY: Teresa SEEMS to have the late 1990s Teen Skipper body mold, but it's actually dated 1998, so I think that it may have been a body mold that is unique to the Very Velvet doll line! Her body almost LOOKS like the bodies of both Skipper and the narrow-hipped Shani dolls; she has a fairly modest-looking bustline, a slender, but less impossible waistline (Which DOESN'T twist or turn), and narrow hips. Her legs arms are the Shani arms, and her bendable legs are slender, ending in high-heel-poised feet. She has a rear end, but it doesn't really stick out. She also has an ever so slight, cool-looking belly button! I personally think that she looks sort of modest, graceful, and pretty.

TERESA'S FACE: Teresa is an intriguing beauty! She has reddish-brown eyebrows...and BLUE eyes! Her lips are a medium nude shade of pink; that is, they're a brownish pink color. Her pretty, blue eyes are lined in black liner, and her lower lids sport iridescent, light mustard yellow shadow, while her upper lids are adorned with smoky brown shadow, and then, higher up, nude-pink shadow. I love Teresa's lovely, toothpaste grin.

TERESA'S HAIR: During the late 1990s, crimped hair returned as a fashion statement, and Teresa is the ONLY Very Velvet doll to wear her hair in this style. Her hair is a medium reddish-brown color, and it is strategically streaked with a medium golden color. Her golden bangs are interesting, because they are straight, flat, thin, and slightly wispy. The bottom half of her hair is all reddish-brown, and it is, more or less, totally crimped. The upper half of her hair is streaked with golden blonde, and it's only crimped on the ends; it's pulled up into a hairband, which is more than likely to have deteriorated by now. A silvery, pointy-edged, crown-type headband is velcro-secured underneath the upper half of Teresa's hair, as though said headband is a delicate tiara; I love the effect that the headband creates in Teresa's hair.

TERESA'S OUTFIT: Teresa's sleek, black, strappy velour bodysuit CAN be removed, but, in order to do so, one must snip the stitches that hold the back of the suit together, and then one must slowly strip the suit off of Teresa...from top to bottom, being careful to NOT stress the tiny, black stockings that are sewn onto the bottom of each leg!

For a cool party look, Teresa can top her bodysuit with her black skirt, which features a wide, ribbon waistband (You tie this soft, shiny waistband into a bow yourself)...and TONS of sparkly black tulle-type fabric! You can tell which side of the skirt should face outward, because it's the SPARKLIEST side! :)

For formal affairs, Teresa can slip into her black, velour-type jacket, which features long sleeves, two tiny, clear, fake rhinestone "buttons", and a REAL snap closure at the top (Does Mattel even MAKE that type of classy closure anymore? LOL!).

Teresa's lovely, black ankle boots feature slightly square toes, chunky high heels, and a VELOUR-TYPE coating, which gives them an elegant, Wintery look; this doll is so old that the "inside" sides of the outsides of each boot will be worn, due to the fact that the two boots were touching each other for so many years. You might want Teresa to wear each boot on the same foot that she was wearing it on when she arrived to you, so that the worn areas won't be so visible.

Teresa wears no jewelry except for a pair of silvery earrings, which really look quite elegant on her.

TERESA'S ACCESSORIES: Of course, a black, oval Barbie brush is on hand, so that you can style and re-style Teresa's beautiful hair, but there are several OTHER accessories, too, including three pens (yellow, lilac, and green) and three sheets of black, velour-type stickers.

THE GOOD: I love Teresa's overall look. She totally embodies the elegant side of the Goth fashion statement that was popular during the late 1990s...by hinting at it ever so slightly; she IS the Goth of the Very Velvet set! She has a regal, royal look to her, and her hair and eye color combination is interesting and lovely.

I also love the sticker sheets! The roses, butterflies, birds, and hearts that are included remind me of romance and beauty.

THE BAD: The sheer AGE of this doll creates most of her "Bad" points. I cannot vouch for the usefulness of the 17-year-old, water-soluble markers, which are SUPPOSED to be used when you're coloring in the white sections of the included stickers. I cannot vouch for the ability of the stickers to stay on Teresa's skin / outfit, either.

I'm a little disappointed by the fact that when I cut the back seam of Teresa's bodysuit (in order to remove it), I accidentally removed a few too many stitches! ACK! If YOU decide to remove Teresa's bodysuit from her, be extremely careful when you're removing those black stitches from that black fabric!

In order to get Teresa's head away from the cardboard box liner, you will have to cut two little, plastic pieces that are attached to her head...AND to the cardboard; this may be extremely tricky, so I would recommend using tiny scissors...You may have to push the remaining ends of these plastic pieces back into Teresa's head.

I love Teresa's interesting torso, but some people might not like the fact that even though this Teresa doll has bendable legs, she DOESN'T have a Twist N' Turn waist.

THE UGLY: The most OBVIOUS "ugly" aspect of this doll is the fact that her boots will be worn from touching each other for 17 years.

The SECOND most obvious ugly aspect of Teresa is truly, truly UGLY! When I just removed Teresa's JACKET, I found that the jacket ITSELF had left a stain on the upper part of Teresa's chest! YUCK! If you remove the entire bodysuit, you'll find that there are stains on the upper parts of her shoulders, on her chest, on her ribcage area, on her lower abdomen, on her back, on her rear end, and ALL OVER HER LEGS! These stains appear to be sort of purply (on her torso) and grey (on her legs). I have not yet tried to remove them. A few people have discussed the removal of these stains online, and so, should you wish to remove any stains that YOUR doll might have sustained over the course of 17 years, please be sure to investigate cleaning methods BEFORE you try anything on your doll, as I do NOT know what certain stain-removing solutions will do to the skin of these Very Velvet dolls.

The third ugly aspect of Teresa concerns factory flaws. My Teresa's right eye seems to be slightly larger, and slightly higher up than her right eye, and the black area of her right eye is a wee bit marred by some extra white paint...ICK! I may have to cover the white flaws with some black paint.

RECOMMENDATIONS: This doll cannot be recommended for children under the age of 3, and I DON'T believe that children should be given this doll...if the bodysuit has been removed, as I think that the sight of the doll's stained body ALONE could sorely disappoint a little kid. That having been said, I can recommend this doll to the following people:

* BARBIE COLLECTORS: The serious Barbie collector might want to add this VERY 1990s Very Velvet Teresa doll to their collection, because she reflects Minimalism, elegance, and a slight Goth sensibility, too.

* COLLECTORS OF ALL THINGS FROM THE 1990s: Anybody who loves, loves, LOVES memorabilia from the 1990s will LOVE this Teresa doll, for the same reasons that Barbie doll collectors will love her!

* GIFT-GIVERS: This elegant, beautiful, decadent Teresa doll would make a cool Valentine's Day gift for that favorite Barbie enthusiast, or a perfect Birthday gift, or even a really classy treat for Halloween! Don't forget those Winter holidays, either! Teresa might be the CLASSIEST gift under the tree next year!

* DECORATORS: Want to add an interesting centerpiece to the table? This Teresa doll might add a truly festive look for a children's party, for Halloween, or just for the Holidays.

* CUSTOMIZERS: If you love to customize Barbie dolls, this elegant Teresa doll might be for YOU! She has an interesting body shape, a cool hair and eye color combination, and an amazing sense of style! If you'd like to do so, you could always try to remove the staining from her skin, in order to ready her for new outfits. If you love to sew, you might try making a few more pieces for her, so that she can wear even more mix n' match outfit looks!

IN CONCLUSION: Georgeous, elegant, Minimalist Very Velvet Teresa ISN'T perfect, but she IS lovely in some very unique ways, and she's sure to make a statement in your world!
top_rant.title: This flawed, romantic, slightly Goth young lady is a true 1990s princess!

OK SO I HAVE BEEN GETTING BACK INTO THIS HOBBY OVER THE PAST YEAR . I USED TO BE AN AVID R/C HOBBYIST ABOUT 10 YRS AGO , AND I AM REALLY IMPRESSED WITH WHAT'S OUT THERE NOW .
SO WHEN I SAW A NICE LOOKING 1/6 SCALE TRUCK FROM A SEEMINGLY REPUTABLE COMPANY FOR UNDER $400 .. I WAS EXCITED .
I DEBATED SPENDING MY MONEY ON THIS TRUCK VS AN ARRMA PRODUCT ( WHICH NEVER DISAPPOINTS ) AND I FIGURED .. LET’S TRY SOMETHING NEW. WHAT I GOT IS AN ARTIFICIALLY ENLARGED COPY OF A CHEAP $30 KIDS TOYS !!! . EVRYTHING IN THIS TRUCK IS SOMETHING THAT RESEMBLES A HOBBY GRADE FEATURE/PART BUT IN REALITY IS A FLIMSY COPY THAT SIMPLY DOESN'T CUT IT!!
IF YOU'RE GONNA MAKE A 1/6 SCALE TRUCK YOU CAN'T PUT IN CHEAP, WEAK PLASTIC COMPONENTS,... WELL I GUESS YOU CAN .... AND IT WILL LOOK REAL NICE , BUT THEN CRACK OR BREAK AS SOON AS IT " TAPS " A CURB .. YES , BROKE A FRONT STEERING ROD ON THE VERY FIRST DRIVE WHEN I LITERALLY JUST BUMPED INTO A CURB. OK , I FIGURED ... WELL YOU GET WHAT YOU PAY FOR , A 1/6 SCALE TRUCK FOR $350 .. IT’S NOT THE END OF THE WORLD IF I HAVE TO REPLACE A PART SO ILL JUST FIX IT .. SO I SPEND A LITTLE MONEY AND TIME AND I FIX IT ,, OOOOOHHHH EXCITED FOR MY SECOND DRIVE... BOOOOMMM WHOLE SERVO BLOWS ,,, UGHHHHHH , OK OK GIVE IT ANOTHER CHANCE ... I REPLACE THE SERVO ,,,, YAAYYYYY HERE WE GO THIRD DRIVE ... LET'S FINALLY HAVE SOME FUN ....... WHAAAAAMMMM ESC BLOWS !!!!!!!! AND THAT'S ALL FOLKS !!!!!!!!!!!! ON THE SHELF NEXT TO THE BROKEN TERROMOTO FROM LAST MONTH ... BACK TO ARRMA !!!!!! THE ONLY THING THESE RED KITTEN GUYS GOT SOMEWHAT RIGHT IS THE
TR-MT10E AND 8 E . BUT I GUESS IT'S JUST .. YOU GET WHAT YOU PAY FOR IN THIS WORLD . THERE IS A PLACE IN THE MARKET FOR A MIDDLE RANGE ( QUALITY WISE) PRODUCT FOR BEGINNERS TO GET THEM INTO THE HOBBY . BUT FOR MY MONEY ... PUT IN A FEW MORE DOLLARS AND GET THE RIGHT THING ( WHSPERRR KRATONNNN)

ADDENDUM...
I SEE ALL THESE OTHER POSITIVE REVIEWS BUT PLEASE UNDERSTAND.....
OVER THE PAST YEAR I’VE BOUGHT 11 TRUCKS INCLUDING TWO RED CAT PRODUCTS BESIDE THIS SHREDDER, ( I bought a TR-MT10E and the 1/8 scale TERRAMOTO BEFORE THIS SHREDDER) THE REST WERE ARRMA AND ONE TRAXXAS .
AND WHAT BOTHERED ME MOST WAS THAT I ACTUALLY EMAILED RED CAT THINKING MAYBE I GOT A BAD UNIT BUT THEIR RESPONSE WAS NOT VERY HELPFUL, AND I AM VERY BUSY, TOO BUSY TO GO BACK AND FORTH WITH EMAILS TO GET THROUGH TO SOMEONE AT THE COMPANY THAT ACTUALLY “WANTS “ TO HELP . I JUST WANT TO BUY MY “ TOY “ AND PLAY WITH IT DURING THE VERY LITTLE FREE LEISURE TIME I HAVE AND NOT DEAL WITH HASSLES. AND I DONT MIND SPENDING EXTRA $$ FOR THIS. SO I GUESS IT DEPENDS ON WHAT YOU ARE LOOKING FOR, IF YOU’RE LIKE ME ARRMA WON’T DISAPPOINT. BUT IF YOU WANT SOMETHING A BIT MORE PRICE FRIENDLY AND DON’T MIND POSSIBLY HAVING TO JUMP THROUGH SOME HOOPS WITH REPLACING PARTS OR NEGOTIATIONS WITH CUSTOMER SERVICE DEPARTMENTS.. THEN THESE RED CAT PRODUCTS HAVE THEIR PLACE . BUT TRY TO AVOID THIS SHREDDER THING . GET THE TR-MT10E . !!

MY GRANDBABY IS 2 BUT VERY PETITE FOR HER AGE, HER FEET ALMOST REACH THE PEDALS ALL THE WAY. MIND YOU, SHE IS 2 AND WEARS A SIZE 2T OR 24 MONTHS - THE EXACT SIZE FOR HER AGE. I'M A MOM OF 3 AND THE BOYS WERE WEARING 24 MONTHS AT AGE 2. IF YOUR LITTLE ONE IS LIKE MOST TODDLERS AND WEARS A LARGER SIZE CLOTHING THAN THEIR AGE OR HAS LONG LEGS. AGE 2 IS FINE TO START. IN A FEW MONTHS OR SO MY GRANDDAUGHTER WILL BE ABLE YO REACH AND SHE JUST TURNED 2 ONE WEEK AGO.
****A WORD TO NEW PARENTS. DON'T PURCHASE YOUR SMALL CHILD A BATTERY OPERATED CAR OR ANY OTHER TYPE OF RIDING DEVICE THAT DOESN'T REQUIRE PEDALING UNTIL THEY LEARN TO RIDE A BIKE - MY DAUGHTER WAS RIDING AT AGE 4 WITH NO TRAINING WHEELS AND CONFIDENTLY. SHE LEARNED AT 3 1/2 YEARS OLD. WE MADE THIS MISTAKE WITH OUR YOUNGEST SON. WE BOUGHT HIM A CAR AT 3 & REFUSED TO RIDE A BIKE AGAIN. DIDN'T END UP LEARNING UNTIL AGE 12 AND ONLY BECAUSE OF OUR INSISTENCE. TODAY'S KIDS WITH THE W0RLD AT THEIR FINGERTIPS (MINE BEGAN USING A TABLET FOR LEARNING GAMES) - HE'S IN HS AND HALF OF HIS PEERS CAN'T EVEN RIDE A BIKE. YOUR CHOICE BUT IF YOU WANT A KID THAT ENJOYS PLAYING OUTDOORS, KEEP THEM AWAY FROM THE BATTERY CHARGED TRANSPORTATION UNTIL THEY RIDE A BIKE WELL AND ENJOY IT. IN MY EXPERIENCE - THE YOUNGER THE BETTER BECAUSE IT'S EASIER FOR THEM TO LEARN AND THEIR FEARS AREN'T FULLY DEVELOPED. THEY SELL VERY SMALL BIKES SO IF YOUR CHILD IS BIG ENOUGH TO WEAR A 3, START THEM USING THIS PRODUCT. THE BACK WHEELS SLIDE OUT FOR GROWTH AND DON''T BE AFRAID TO GET YOUR 3 YEAR OLD ON A SMALL LITTLE BIKE. MY DAUGHTER'S FATHER PUT A HELMET & KNEE PADS ON MY DAUGHTER AND NEVER BOTHERED WITH THE TRAINING WHEELS. HER BALANCE WAS WELL DEVELOPED FROM USING HER TRICYCLE. DON'T HESITATE. NO KID SHOULD MISS THAT EXPERIENCE OF RIDING THEIR BIKE AROUND. THAT 1ST FEELING OF FREEDOM - OBVIOUSLY A 4 YEAR OLD CAN'T HAVE FREEDOM BUT BY LEARNING SO EARLY SHE DEVELOPED A LOVE FOR BEING OUTDOORS & PLAYING & LATER THE FREEDOM OF THE BIKE, TAKE IT FROM SOMEONE WHO HAS 3 KIDS. MY YOUNGEST, BCUZ OF ELECTRONICS AND A BATTERY CHARGED VEHICLE BEFORE HE LEARNED THE BEAUTY OF OUTDOOR ACTIVITY, THE WORLD IS VERY DIFFERENT NOW & SINCE THE TECH REVOLUTION, MANY MANY KIDS ARE MISSING OUT ON THE JOYS OF PLAYING OUTSIDE

top_screaming.text:
DEFECTIVE PRODUCT. THIS IS THE DEFINITION OF A FLAWED AND DEFECTIVE PRODUCT THAT SHOULD BE REMOVED FROM THE MARKET IMMEDIATELY. NO ETHICAL COMPANY WOULD CONTINUE TO SELL THIS PRODUCT KNOWING THAT IT IS BEING MANUFACTURED (I THINK IT IS CONTRACT MANUFACTURED IN CHINA FOR MELISSA AND DOUG) WITH SUCH POOR WORKMANSHIP.

I HAVE BUILT HUNDREDS OF TOYS, BIKES, FURNITURE, RADIO CONTROL CARS FROM SCRATCH ETC. AND HAVE NEVER ENCOUNTERED SOMETHING LIKE THIS. ALMOST EVERY SCREW HOLE/INSERT WAS MISALIGNED, DAMAGED, AND/OR POORLY DRILLED. SCREW HOLES WERE NOT THREADED PROPERLY. SOME OF THE BOLTS WOULD NOT TIGHTEN NO MATTER WHAT ADJUSTMENTS WERE MADE. IN THE CASE OF THE RED ELEVATOR STRUCTURE, I HAD TO FORCIBLY RE-ALIGN THE THREADED INSERT AS THE SCREW WOULD NOT DRIVE IN PROPERLY AND COULD NOT BE SECURED. IT TOOK ME OVER AN HOUR TO BUILD (AND FIX) SOMETHING THAT SHOULD HAVE TAKEN 10 MINUTES. ANYBODY WHO THINKS THAT THIS IS A HIGH QUALITY TOY BECAUSE IT IS MADE OUT OF WOOD IS KIDDING THEMSELVES.

LIKE MANY OTHER REVIEWERS, I ALSO FOUND RANDOM GLUE SPOTS ON VARIOUS PARTS (PARKING DECKS) RIGHT OUT OF THE BOX. AGAIN UNREAL THAT A COMPANY WITH ANY QUALITY CONTROL WOULD ALLOW THIS TO GO ON ANY LONGER. I FEEL TERRIBLE FOR THE MANY PARENTS WHO MUST HAVE STRUGGLED BUILDING THIS FOR THEIR CHILDREN.

I HAVEN'T EVEN TOUCHED ON THE RAMPS AND HOW POORLY MADE THEY ARE. SPARE YOURSELF THE TORTURE OF TRYING TO ATTACH THE RAMP SECTIONS WITH THE INCORRECTLY SIZED CONNECTORS AND THE TINY SCREWS WHICH ARE IMPOSSIBLE TO FASTEN BECAUSE OF THE ALIGNMENT ISSUES.

I AM TRULY BLOWN AWAY THAT COMPANIES WILL CONTINUE TO SELL DEFECTIVE PRODUCTS LIKE THIS. MELISSA & DOUG - FIRE YOUR CONTRACT MANUFACTURER - THEY ARE MAKING STAINING YOUR REPUTATION.

top_screaming.text: BOUGHT THIS FOR MY DAUGHTERS 5TH BIRTHDAY. SHE WANTED THE BARBIE HOUSE WITH THE ELEVATOR. AFTER LOOKING AT THE MATTEL PRODUCT WHICH IS MOSTLY PLASTIC, I FOUND THIS ON AMAZON. ORDERED IT FROM VMINNOVATIONS AND THE SPEED AT WHICH THEY DELIVERED IT WAS AMAZING. I ORDERED IT AROUND 9:30 PM WED. NIGHT THANKGIVING EVE AND IT ARRIVED ON SAT. AFTER THANKSGIVING. LESS THAN 3 DAYS ON A HOLIDAY WEEKEND. BOX ARRIVED AND IT WAS WELL PACKED. I READ ANYWHERE BETWEEN 21/2 HOURS TO 5 HOURS TO PUT TOGETHER. DEFFINATELY TAKE YOUR TIME, BECAUSE THE INSTRUCTIONS DONT SHOW YOU WHICH SIDE OF SOME OF THE PANELS TO USE. A LITTLE ATTENTION TO DETAIL AND COMMON SENSE HELPS HERE. I FOLLOWED SOME OTHER REVIEWERS ADVICE AND USED WOOD GLUE WHEN ASSEMBLING AND THIS THING IS VERY STURDY. DEFFINATELY WILL HOLD UP TO HOURS OF PLAY TIME. THE FURNITURE IS VERY CUTE, EVERYTHING IS MADE OF WOOD AND HAS CUSHIONING AND FABRIC DETAIL. I HIGHLY RECOMMEND THIS KIDKRAFT PRODUCT OVER THE MATTEL HOUSE. AS FOR SOME OF THE REVIEWERS MENTIONING THE ELEVATOR BECOMING HARD TO USE I HAVE A SIMPLE SOLUTION. REMOVE THE CAP FROM THE ELEVATOR RAIL AND REMOVE THE ELEVATOR SLIDERS. YOU WILL SEE A METAL BRACKET ON THE FACE OF THE SLIDERS. CVAREFULLY REMOVE THIS CAP THAT HOLDS A BALL BEARING AND A SPRING SIMILAR TO A BALL POINT PEN SPRING. WITH A SMALL PAIR OF SNIPS, REMOVE A FEW COILS OF THE SPRING AND REASSEMBLE SLIDERS. THIS WILL ALLOW THE ELEVATOR TO MOVE MORE FREELY. I MAY HAVE OVERDONE IT BECAUSE THE ELEVATOR SLOWLY FALLS TO THE FIRST FLOOR BUT I THINK IT'S BETTER THAN THE CHILD NOT BEING ABLE TO MOVE THE ELEVATOR AT ALL. IF YOU ARE HESITANT ABOUT BUYING THIS HOUSE OVER THE MATTEL BARBIE HOUSE, DEFINATELY GO WITH THE KIDKRAFT, YOU WILL BE HAPPY YOU DID.
top_screaming.title: KIDKRAFT MUCH BETTER VALUE THAN MATTEL

top_five_star_obscene.text:
My 5-year-old has been watching Octonauts for a few years, and it's one of the few kids' shows I actually like. As a rule, I hate most modern kids' programming, especially Nick crap, but even a lot of the PBS shows. Most kids' shows are garbage--ugly, badly written, too focused on characters who are jerks, of just 20-minute commercials for whatever crappy toy. Octonauts, on the other hand, is great. Yes, it ignores most of the food chain, but introduces kids to a myriad of fascinating creatures, promotes conservation and teamwork, poo-poos silly superstitions, and has female characters in smart (if supporting, my only quibble) roles. And it's adorable. Along with Ocean Mysteries & Zoobomafoo, it's really cultivated an enthisiasm for animals and wild nature in my daughter. So, unlike stupid Dora and Disney princesses and the rest, I'm totally happy for her to play with these licensed characters.

I've bought her a few pieces here and there, but it's hard to track down anyone but the 3 male leads in the U.S. This set has the whole octopod, plus a gup, and the whole core gang! It's fun and expansive, and works great with a collection of animal toys, especially marine animals. She already had the gup & barnacles, but she got them when she was perhaps too small, and broke it pretty quickly, so it's fun to have an intact gup back in play (the old Barnacles is long gone). It's a lot if little bits to potentially lose (but a lot fewer than even a wee Legos kit), but also a lot of fun.
top_five_star_obscene.title: The gang's all here! Big set for a great show.

It feels more responsive, whereas before it seemed like the crappy stock shafts were twisting as it accelled. I'm working on fixing it up now, still works after a half year in out florida garage. I have this version of Superman. Got in a 3 pack with Steppenwolf (great build, crap look, I painted him to be screen accurate) and Batman. Much better the 10 dollar BvS inches. Still a weird sculpt of Cavill's face. They never seem to get it right. Cloth Cape, more articulate and vibrant...proportional hands. Reiterate CLOTH Cape not that crap vinyl that came kinked and damaged and just tore away.

DOOMSDAY. This would be worth 50 bucks for me. Because I have all the other stuff, kids have quite the collection and I have a few on my shelf with repaints (5 o'clock shadow on Batman was the bane of my existence for a while lol). But Doomsday. I seen someone make him with a large Hulk figure as a base. I was gonna do the same with a double we had but never got around to it.

The story goes that a prototype of Doomsday leaked and rather then have people judge him based on a picture of a unfinished demo, Snyder decided to pop the trailer out with him in it. Honestly...I was effen suprised and stoked. I actually felt the bigger spoiler was showing and confirming Wonder Woman was in the film. Especially since shes only referred to as Ms Prince and non core fans may not have put 2 and 2 together and her entrance would be even more bad ass.

Anycase it's nice to see them finally out him out. I was bummed they hadn't made him the collect and connect although the completed Grapnel was pretty cool. I would have to repaint and mod him...add some spikes but just to have him...hell yeah.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
This map is incredible. And the case that comes with it is durable and a great value.
It’s worth it to buy it just for those things. I’ve used it for several weeks now and I love it.
However, be aware that you need to buy some markers to go with it. The markers it comes with are pretty much garbage. You can get a nice set of washable Crayola markers at a JoAnns or Michaels for cheap if you like a marker with a wide tip. Or wet erase Expo markers on Amazon if you like a fine tip (my preference). Just remember, DRY ERASE BAD. I also recommend throwing the erasss it comes with in the trash and investing in some dollar store wash cloths and putting a couple cloths in the case for easy cleanup, it’s best to wipe it down with one damp cloth and then wipe it dry with a fresh cloth. The erasers it comes with are very bad and a cloth will clean it perfectly.
The clips it comes with are incredibly flimsy. One of them came broken. So don’t expect a lot out of the crap metal clips it comes with.
Do not let the crummy clips, erasers, or markers dissuade you, this map is incredibly high-quality. Definitely worth the investment. And when considering the cost of buying a separate case for a map, this makes it an extremely valuable purchase for the money and quality. Listen, if you are buying a map, BUY A CASE. I cannot stress that enough. Maps are a money investment. And if you don't buy a case to go with it, inevitably, your map will wind up stored somewhere (probably a closet) and get squished accidentally and now it has fold lines through it FOREVER. I’ve seen all my friends maps meet that end.
Extra Bonus: you don't have to fear this will get crunched in the mail like other maps people order online because it is delivered in that strong, plastic case.

Bottom line: the feel of this map and the clear layer on top is so solid I wouldn’t want to use anything else except maybe a Chessex map. But Chessex doesn’t come in this size. And honestly, I prefer this map with its clear layer because I feel my map is safe from hazy marker cloud stains over time.

tl;dr
10/10 map and case. 1 clip arrived broken, markers and erasers are garbage, but the map and case more than make up for it in quality and money value. Would buy again or gift to friends.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Buy it for the map and the case

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Originally posted at www.throatpunchgames.com, a new idea everyday!

Product-Pathfinder Adventure Card Game—Skull & Shackles Adventure Deck 6: From Hell’s Heart
Producer-Paizo
Price- $ 20 here http://www.amazon.com/Pathfinder-Adventure-Card-Game-Shackles/dp/1601256949/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1444228580&sr=8-1&keywords=From+hell%27s+heart
Set-up/Play/Clean-up- 60 minutes per scenario (1-6 players, 5 scenarios)
Type-American
Depth-medium
TL; DR-The ships paid off! 98%

Basics-Do you have what it takes to take the crown? From Hell’s Heart is the conclusion of the Skull and Shackles Adventure Card Game, and it’s a doozy. Players have to contend with an invasion from the devil worshipers from the north, then fight it out to become rulers of the Shackles. To play, you will need the base Pathfinder Adventure Card Game as well as the first five adventure decks.

Mechanics-SHIPS! If you’ve played the first five adventure decks, you keep getting ships. From the outside, they just look like another way your character can level up as you become increasingly awesome. However, when you get to second scenario in this pack, you get to fight all the ships you DIDN’T take as they are invading the Shackles! That right there makes everything completely forgiven. This adventure pack has many of the same mechanics you’ve come to expect, but the crazy sea battle is just phenomenal. It’s quickly done, easy to pull off, and an absolute blast. This pack isn’t a slouch by the way. No monsters will pull their punches here. You have to earn the crown, and it feel glorious when you do! 5/5

Theme- I keep complaining that this isn’t the most theme orientated American style game out there. However, the mechanics really drive home some of the more awesome points. Sure you don’t get a full introduction of all the people, but the varied encounters and the setup of each scenario really provide the atmosphere that will keep you in the game as a pirate. It’s not perfect, but it’s as good as you’re going to get without a guide. 4.5/5

Instructions- The game has set itself up well, and at this point is just taking a victory lap. All the previous adventure packs and base game set up the pieces and instructions, and this one just knocks it out of the park. All the rules are easy to follow, and the scenarios fall into piece from there. 5/5

Execution-I like the art to this game, the cards are in good shape, and I don’t have to destroy the packaging to get at the components. That’s all I can ask for in a card game! 5/5

Summary-Paizo had me hooked a long time ago when I bought this adventure path’s starter set. Once you’re in for $60, unless something is completely horrible, you’re going to pay the additional $100 across five other packs to get the whole game. Since I was $80 in before the last pack, this one could have been absolute crap and I’d most likely still have bought it to complete my set. However, this one is the best part of the whole path. You have ship battles, you have mass naval combat ( something I didn’t think i’d see!), secret missions into the heart of the fortress, a stalking dragon (that makes the blessing deck a terrible, mind numbing, scary ordeal!), and an epic final battle with the pirate king. This is everything I wanted this AP to be, and I got more. If you were not sure about playing the Pathfinder adventure card game before, this is the reason you play this game. I promise you will enjoy it! 98%
top_five_star_obscene.title: Ring Side Report-Board Game Review of Pathfinder Adventure Card Game—Skull & Shackles Adventure Deck 6: From Hell’s Heart

top_five_star_obscene.text: Our son loves this dump truck. We love how it does not require any batteries and has no noises (we have so many toys that use batteries and have noises). The build quality of this toy is durable and has not broken yet even though our son sometimes plays a little rough with it. Our son hasn't gotten the hang of pushing the top button multiple types (hydraulics) to make the dump truck go up, but he's getting there. The only downside with this toy is that it comes with a blue garbage can that attaches to the back and it falls off of the hinges pretty easily. I don't think it is a big deal though. Our son doesn't even really play with the garbage can. He just enjoys throwing things into the garbage truck. The inside of the garbage truck is holly and can hold a lot of things. Kids will love throwing random things into the garbage truck. Good size truck for toddlers.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Good simple garbage truck that does not need batteries!

top_five_star_obscene.title: Great For Play With Toy Garbage Trucks !!

top_five_star_obscene.text: This is my first non-powered blaster and I must say I'm impressed.  The blaster is small and light weight, but fits my adult hand very well.  It is easy to cock and load and gets decent range for such a simple design (25 to 30 feet with the included whistler darts, also with regular streamline darts, only 15 to 20 feet with suction cup darts, and a nice 35 to 40 feet with the elite darts).  It accecpts a variety of dart styles (I have used WHistlers, Suction cup, Regular Streamlines, and Elites).  There is a nice Top tactical rail and towords the back of the rail is a small button.  Depressing this fires the baster (a very cool feature if your being taken prisoner and have to turn over your blaster, you can get one more shot off without touching the trigger!).  Loading is a simple as can be (cock the gun and push a dart into the barrel).  Ialso love the fact that it holds 2 extra darts below the barrel (a real plus if your running low on darts in the heated part of the battle).  I got my 2 pack on clearence at a local store for ten dollars off what it is here.  Not sure if the price here is a little high as I've only found these at one local store and like I say they were on clearence.  About the only thing I don't like about these blasters is that the slide tends to be a little loose so it rattles somewhat (as most slide blasters do).  All in all these are a cool addition to someone needing a simple sidearm, or for close quarters work where a barrage of darts is not required!
top_five_star_obscene.title: Compact sidearm

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I got this as a fun way to spend my time on the beach. This was cheap enough and had an included camera so I went for it - not expecting much. Holy crap is this thing fun. One of the best investments I made. It flies really well - and has some cool features like doing barrel rolls and having options for low and high speed.

This thing was surviging on the beach with relatively high winds - i was impressed. Of course I lost control of it several times - but it held up through the crashes and sand.

Taking images and videos is a pain on this thing - it is hard to tell when its on or not. Also you have to turn off the camera first before turningn the drone off or else the data will become corrupted. Further, the image and video quality is somewhat shitty. The video - when the drone is stabilized - is OK but there is no gyro or stabilizer for the camera so do not expect much.

If you are looking to fly a drone for the first time this one is great - if you want to dish out extra dough look for drones that have a larger range (100m).
top_five_star_obscene.title: Great beginner drone!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
My first purchase of the Nerf N-Strike Jolt Blaster was strictly based on the small size since the recipient was my 3-year old grandson. I have to admit that I was shocked at the speed of the darts, and not just "it's powerful for its size". It's powerful compared to any Nerf gun regardless of size and cost. So, I bought it again.

I will say that a 3-year old is not strong enough to cock this, so I have to continually cock it for him. No big deal.

I wanted another Jolt before they went up in cost, and at $5.99 I am satisfied that I got a nice deal. The only other gun I considered was the Triad, which is essentially the same size frame with a bit more bulk because you can load 3 darts on it.

You won't be disappointed with the Jolt.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Had to get another Jolt
